verses.text: God speaks from the whirlwind and begins asking Job questions about creation. ‘Knowest thou the time when the wild goats of the rock bring forth?’ The Lord points to the most obscure births in the wilderness — mountain goats labouring alone, far from human eyes. God knows what Job cannot. The argument is not crushing but expanding: trust in a knowledge wider than your own.

‘Canst thou number the months that they fulfil?’ God presses further. The gestation of wild deer, hidden in canyons — even this is known to Him. The question is not rhetorical mockery but invitation: imagine being a God whose attention covers the most remote pregnancies in creation. Such a God, surely, has not forgotten you.

‘They bow themselves, they bring forth their young ones, they cast out their sorrows.’ The wild creatures labour and deliver, then leave their birth-pains behind. The image is tender. Even in their pain, the animals do not know who watches over them, yet they are watched. The same God watches Job, even when Job feels unwatched.

‘Their young ones are in good liking; they grow up with corn; they go forth, and return not unto them.’ The Lord describes the natural course of wild young: nursed, grown, and gone. Creation is full of these self-sufficient lives. God’s care reaches into wildernesses no one curates. If He sustains animals He never sees domesticated, surely He has not abandoned the man bowed before Him.

burro selvagem... odeia (literalmente despreza ) o barulho da cidade: Este é o primeiro de uma série de animais que desprezam outros que são inferiores de alguma forma (cp. **39.18** , **22** ; **41.29** ). Estas imagens ilustram como Deus despreza a oposição de um homem como Jó (veja **Sl 2.4** ).\n\n

Agora extinto e já raro na época de Moisés, o boi selvagem era o animal terrestre mais poderoso no início de Canaã. Este símbolo de força do Antigo Testamento ( **Nm 23.22** ; **24.8** ; **Dt 33.17** ; **Sl 29.6** ; **92.10** ) nunca foi domesticado (cp. **Gn 1.28** ; **9.2** ; **Sl 8.5-6** ).\n\n
